content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 33 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 118r–119v | **Source Images:** DEU_033_page_001.jpg, DEU_033_page_002.jpg, DEU_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

*[Moses' Blessing of the Tribes — A poem of benediction, the last words of Moses over the people he has led for forty years. Like Jacob's blessing in Genesis 49, this poem moves tribe by tribe, but Moses speaks as prophet-poet, not dying patriarch. PNEUMA renders it with the force and lyricism the Hebrew demands.]*

---

**1** This is the blessing that Moses the man of God pronounced over the Israelites before his death.

**2**
He said:

The LORD came from Sinai,
    he rose like the sun from Seir;
he shone forth from Mount Paran.
    He came from Meribah Kadesh
and from the south, along the slopes
    with flaming fire at his right hand.

**3**
Surely he loves his people!
    All his holy ones are in your hands.
They bow down at your feet
    and receive your instruction —

**4**
the instruction Moses placed before us,
    the inheritance of the assembly of Jacob.

**5**
He was king in Jeshurun,
    when the leaders of the people gathered,
when all the tribes of Israel came together.

---

**Reuben**

**6**
Let Reuben live and not die,
    though his people be few.

---

**Judah**

**7**
And this of Judah he said:

Hear, LORD, the cry of Judah;
    bring him to his people.
With his hands he contends for himself —
    be a help against his foes!

---

**Levi**

**8**
Of Levi he said:

Your Thummim and Urim belong to your faithful servant,
    whom you tested at Massah,
with whom you contended at the waters of Meribah.

**9**
He said of his father and mother, "I do not know them."
    He did not acknowledge his brothers
or recognize his children —
    for he observed your word
and kept your covenant.

**10**
He teaches your regulations to Jacob
    and your instruction to Israel.
He offers incense before you
    and whole burnt offerings on your altar.

**11**
Bless all his skills, LORD,
    and be pleased with the work of his hands.
Crush the loins of those who rise against him;
    strike his enemies down, so they rise no more!

---

**Benjamin**

**12**
Of Benjamin he said:

Let the beloved of the LORD rest secure in him,
    for he shields him all day long;
the one the LORD loves rests between his shoulders.

---

**Joseph**

**13**
Of Joseph he said:

May the LORD bless his land
    with the precious gift of dew from heaven above,
    and of the deep waters below —

**14**
with the best that the sun brings forth,
    the finest that the moon can yield —

**15**
with the choicest gifts of the ancient mountains
    and the abundance of the everlasting hills —

**16**
with the finest gifts of the earth and its fullness,
    and the favor of him who dwelt in the burning bush.
Let all this rest on the head of Joseph,
    on the brow of the prince among his brothers.

**17**
In majesty he is like a firstborn bull;
    his horns are the horns of a wild ox.
With them he will gore the nations,
    driving them to the ends of the earth.
Such are the ten thousands of Ephraim,
    and such are the thousands of Manasseh.

---

**Zebulun and Issachar**

**18**
Of Zebulun he said:

Rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out,
    and you, Issachar, in your tents.

**19**
They will summon peoples to the mountain;
    there they will offer the right sacrifices.
For they will feast on the abundance of the seas,
    on the treasures hidden in the sand.

---

**Gad**

**20**
Of Gad he said:

Blessed is he who enlarges Gad's territory!
    Gad lives there like a lion,
tearing at an arm or a head.

**21**
He chose the best land for himself;
    the leader's portion was kept for him.
When the heads of the people gathered,
    he carried out the LORD's righteous will,
his judgments concerning Israel.

---

**Dan**

**22**
Of Dan he said:

Dan is a lion's cub
    that leaps out from Bashan.

---

**Naphtali**

**23**
Of Naphtali he said:

Naphtali, overflowing with favor,
    full of the blessing of the LORD —
take possession of the west and the south.

---

**Asher**

**24**
Of Asher he said:

Most blessed of sons is Asher!
    May he be the favorite among his brothers,
bathing his feet in oil.

**25**
The bolts of your gates will be iron and bronze,
    and your strength will match your days.

---

**Closing Doxology**

**26**
There is no one like the God of Jeshurun,
    who rides the heavens to help you,
the clouds in his majesty.

**27**
The eternal God is your refuge;
    underneath are his everlasting arms.
He drove out your enemy before you
    and commanded: "Destroy them!"

**28**
So Israel lives in safety;
    Jacob's spring is secure,
in a land of grain and new wine,
    where the heavens drop their dew.

**29**
How blessed are you, O Israel!
    Who is like you, a people saved by the LORD?
He is your shield and helper,
    your glorious sword.
Your enemies will cower before you,
    and you will tread on their backs.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Overall structure — Tribal blessings:** Moses' tribal poem parallels Jacob's in Genesis 49, but the tone is warmer and more hopeful throughout. Where Jacob's blessing often recalled past failures (Reuben, Simeon, Levi), Moses' largely looks forward in blessing. Simeon is notably absent — likely absorbed into Judah by the time of this poem's composition.

**V. 2 — Theophany opening:** The poem opens not with a blessing but with a cosmic theophany — the LORD marching from Sinai/Seir/Paran, fire at his right hand. This is ancient war-march poetry (compare Judges 5, Habakkuk 3). PNEUMA renders the progression of images as a dawn-rise: "came / rose like the sun / shone forth" — light imagery building to fire.

**V. 3 — "All his holy ones":** qedoshim (holy ones) likely refers to the angelic host accompanying the LORD — the same divine council as in Deuteronomy 32:8. PNEUMA renders it literally; the image of Israel as beloved children held in the hands of the divine host is extraordinary.

**V. 5 — "He was king in Jeshurun":** The referent is ambiguous — it could be the LORD who was king, or Moses in his role as covenant mediator. PNEUMA leaves the ambiguity intact by using "He" without specifying, letting the reader receive the double resonance.

**V. 6 — Reuben:** The briefest blessing, barely a blessing at all. "Let Reuben live and not die" — a thin edge of survival. The tribe that lost the birthright (Gen 49:3-4) receives minimal benediction. "Though his people be few" may already reflect Reuben's diminishment in the historical memory of Israel.

**V. 8 — Thummim and Urim:** The priestly oracular objects are given their Hebrew order here (Thummim first) — unusual, since most texts say "Urim and Thummim." PNEUMA follows the Hebrew text of Deuteronomy exactly. These were the lot-casting devices by which priests discerned divine will.

**V. 9 — Levi's devotion:** The blessing of Levi recalls the Levites' action at Massah/Meribah and (most likely) the golden calf incident (Exodus 32:27-29), when Levi took up the sword against even family members in fidelity to the LORD. PNEUMA renders "I do not know them" as a stark, fierce statement of theological priority — not callousness, but consecration.

**V. 12 — Benjamin — "between his shoulders":** The image of the LORD as one who carries Benjamin between his shoulders (or: Benjamin dwelling between the shoulders/peaks of the LORD) is geographically evocative — Jerusalem sits on Benjamin's tribal territory, between the hills. The phrase may describe both the shelter of mountains and the carrying embrace of God. PNEUMA renders "rests between his shoulders" to preserve the tender, physical warmth of the image.

**Vv. 13-16 — Joseph's blessing (the longest):** The elaborate catalog of natural gifts — dew, deep waters, sun, moon, mountains, hills, earth's fullness — climaxes with a reference to "him who dwelt in the burning bush" (ha-shokhen ba-sneh). This is a unique epithet for God, anchoring Joseph's prosperity in the Exodus revelation. PNEUMA preserves "burning bush" as the full phrase rather than softening to "the bush."

**V. 17 — "Wild ox" (re'em):** The re'em (often mistranslated "unicorn" in older traditions) is the wild aurochs — the great wild bull. PNEUMA uses "wild ox" for accuracy. Ephraim and Manasseh as the ten thousands and thousands — their combined size makes them the dominant tribes of the northern kingdom.

**V. 26 — "God of Jeshurun":** The unique title El Yeshurun ties the blessing's close back to the opening and to the Song of Moses (32:15). Israel at its best is Jeshurun — the upright one, the beloved people.

**V. 27 — "Everlasting arms":** me'onah 'elohei qedem u-mitachat zero'ot olam — "The dwelling of the eternal God, and underneath — arms of eternity." PNEUMA renders "everlasting arms" as the established English phrase that has become an icon of devotional poetry. This verse has sustained more mourners and exiles than perhaps any other in Deuteronomy.

**V. 29 — "ashreikhah Yisrael" — "How blessed are you!":** The poem closes with the same note that opens the Psalter (Psalm 1:1 — ashrei). Israel is not merely fortunate; they are the recipients of divine favor beyond all nations. PNEUMA ends with the exultant military image of enemies cowering and Israel treading on their backs — the war poem of chapter 32 answered by a victory shout.

— PNEUMA
